children.name: Gekko
children.description: True geckos.

children.name: Gekko gecko
children.description: Largest common gecko at 35 cm; loud 'to-KAY' call gives its name; extremely powerful toe pads using van der Waals forces; aggressive bite.

children.name: Gekko vittatus
children.description: SE Asian arboreal gecko with white dorsal stripe.

children.name: Gekko japonicus
children.description: Hardy gecko reaching its northernmost distribution in Japan; commonly found on buildings in temperate regions; tolerates cooler temperatures than most geckos.

children.name: Gekko smithii
children.description: Vivid green arboreal gecko from Southeast Asia; one of the larger Gekko species reaching 20 cm; popular in the exotic pet trade.

children.name: Gekko kikuchii
children.description: Taiwan endemic gecko restricted to lowland and mid-elevation forests; named for Japanese naturalist Yojiro Kikuchi; listed as vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Gekko grossmanni
children.description: Rare gecko from limestone karst habitats in Cambodia and Vietnam; distinguished by its warty skin texture; threatened by quarrying of its karst habitat.

children.name: Gekko mindorensis
children.description: Philippine endemic gecko named for Mindoro island; found in primary and secondary forests; emits a distinctive series of clicking calls.

children.name: Gekko taiwaniensis
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan from lowlands to 1500 m; emits a distinctive two-note call.

children.name: Gekko hokkaensis
children.description: The northernmost Gekko; hibernates in deep rock crevices through cold winters.

children.name: Gekko liberatus
children.description: Restricted to primary lowland forest in the Sierra Madre of the Philippines.

children.name: Gekko shibatai

children.name: Hemidactylus
children.description: Cosmopolitan house geckos.

children.name: Hemidactylus frenatus
children.description: The world's most widespread gecko; has colonised human buildings globally following shipping routes; hunts insects around lights at night.

children.name: Hemidactylus turcicus
children.description: Pink translucent skin through which internal organs are visible; introduced globally; nocturnal on buildings.

children.name: Hemidactylus mabouia
children.description: African origin but now widespread in the Americas through human transport; common on buildings throughout the Caribbean and South America; aggressive competitor displacing native geckos.

children.name: Hemidactylus platyurus
children.description: Distinctive flattened tail used for camouflage against tree bark; native to Southeast Asia but introduced to Florida; hunts on tree trunks and buildings at night.

children.name: Hemidactylus brookii
children.description: Widespread tropical gecko introduced to many regions via global trade; parthenogenetic populations exist in some areas; commonly found on walls and under bark.

children.name: Hemidactylus garnotii
children.description: All-female parthenogenetic species; widespread across Southeast Asia and Pacific islands; flattened body allows it to hide in narrow crevices; named for French naturalist Garnot.

children.name: Hemidactylus robustus
children.description: Stocky East African house gecko; common on buildings and in gardens; formerly considered a subspecies of H. mabouia but now recognized as distinct.

children.name: Hemidactylus flaviviridis
children.description: Large house gecko from South Asia and the Middle East; yellowish ventral coloration distinguishes it; common on buildings in arid regions.

children.name: Hemidactylus freycinneti
children.description: Small gecko from Southeast Asia and northern Australia; found under bark and in leaf litter as well as on buildings; named for French explorer Louis de Freycinet.

children.name: Hemidactylus mercatorius
children.description: Small Madagascar and East African gecko; reproduces by parthenogenesis; commonly found on buildings and in gardens; named after cartographer Gerardus Mercator.

children.name: Hemidactylus angulatus
children.description: A West African species formerly confused with H. mabouia; distinguished by angular scale rows.

children.name: Hemidactylus tenuicaudus
children.description: A Sri Lankan endemic with an exceptionally slender whiplike tail.

children.name: Hemidactylus grenadiensis
children.description: Endemic to Grenada; one of few native Hemidactylus in the Caribbean.

children.name: Hemidactylus imbricatus
children.description: From the Western Ghats of India with overlapping tile-like scale arrangement.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus
children.description: Bent-toed geckos, a species-rich Asian genus of ground and rock geckos with curved toe pads.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus pulchellus
children.description: Strikingly patterned bent-toed gecko from Southeast Asia; found on limestone karst formations and in lowland forests; beautiful dorsal banding makes it popular with collectors.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus marmoratus
children.description: Marbled patterning on grey-brown background; widespread across Southeast Asian islands; nocturnal ground-dweller in forests and gardens.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus brevipalmatus
children.description: Thai-Malay Peninsula endemic with notably short digits; inhabits lowland and hill forests; one of many Cyrtodactylus species described from karst habitats.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus consobrinus
children.description: Borneo endemic bent-toed gecko; closely related to C. marmoratus; found in primary and secondary forests on the island.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus macrotuberculatus
children.description: Distinguished by prominent enlarged tubercles on the dorsal surface; Southeast Asian forest dweller; found on rock faces and tree trunks at night.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus louisiadensis
children.description: Endemic to the Louisiade Archipelago off Papua New Guinea; one of few Cyrtodactylus species from Melanesia; inhabits tropical forest and coastal habitats.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus sadleiri
children.description: Christmas Island endemic; one of two gecko species restricted to this Indian Ocean island; threatened by habitat degradation and introduced predators.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus darmandvillei
children.description: Indonesian bent-toed gecko from the Lesser Sunda Islands; named for a Dutch colonial figure; inhabits monsoon forest and rocky outcrops.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus phaya
children.description: Microendemic to a single limestone karst tower in Kayah State, Myanmar; Critically Endangered by quarrying.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus francoi
children.description: A Thai endemic from Phitsanulok limestone hills.

children.name: Cyrtodactylus langui
children.description: A Vietnamese cave-adapted species with reduced eyes and pale coloration.

children.name: Phelsuma
children.description: Brilliantly coloured Malagasy day geckos.

children.name: Phelsuma madagascariensis
children.description: Brilliant green with red dorsal spots; diurnal and conspicuous unlike most geckos; laps nectar from flowers; important pollinator.

children.name: Phelsuma laticauda
children.description: Introduced to Hawaii; charismatic green with gold speckling.

children.name: Phelsuma grandis
children.description: One of the largest day geckos reaching 30 cm; bright green with red head markings; popular in captivity due to its size and vivid coloration; native to northern Madagascar.

children.name: Phelsuma lineata
children.description: Smaller Phelsuma with distinctive dark lateral lines on green background; widespread across Madagascar's central highlands; several subspecies adapted to different elevations.

children.name: Phelsuma quadriocellata
children.description: Named for four conspicuous ocelli on the flanks; iridescent green with red and blue markings; one of the most colourful Phelsuma species from Madagascar's east coast.

children.name: Phelsuma standingi
children.description: Largest Phelsuma species reaching 30 cm; grey-green with turquoise highlights; restricted to dry deciduous forest in southwest Madagascar; named for Reverend Standing.

children.name: Phelsuma cepediana
children.description: Mauritius endemic with brilliant blue tail and green body; important pollinator of native Mauritian flora; threatened by habitat loss and invasive predators.

children.name: Phelsuma guimbeaui
children.description: Critically endangered Mauritius endemic restricted to a few coastal islets; saved from extinction by captive breeding and reintroduction; named for Mauritian naturalist Guimbeau.

children.name: Phelsuma abbotti
children.description: Found in Comoros and East African coast; less brightly coloured but an important pollinator.

children.name: Phelsuma borbonica
children.description: Endemic to Reunion Island; two subspecies with distinct red or blue spot patterns.

children.name: Phelsuma gigas
children.description: An extinct giant day gecko from Rodrigues Island reaching 40 cm.

children.name: Uroplatus
children.description: Masterful Malagasy camoufleurs.

children.name: Uroplatus phantasticus
children.description: Extraordinary camouflage mimicking dead leaves; irregular skin edges eliminate shadow; named for its supraorbital horn-like projections; Madagascar endemic.

children.name: Uroplatus sikorae
children.description: Flattens itself against bark, spreading the body margin to eliminate shadow; masters of bark mimicry.

children.name: Uroplatus fimbriatus
children.description: Largest leaf-tailed gecko reaching 30 cm; fringed skin margins break up body outline against tree bark; Madagascar's eastern rainforest endemic; opens bright orange-red mouth when threatened.

children.name: Uroplatus ebenaui
children.description: One of the smallest Uroplatus species; short rounded tail unlike the leaf-shaped tails of larger relatives; Madagascar dry forest endemic; named for German zoologist Ebenau.

children.name: Uroplatus lineatus
children.description: Distinctive dark longitudinal lines on the body; Madagascar east coast rainforest species; closely related to U. fimbriatus but smaller and more slender.

children.name: Uroplatus guentheri
children.description: Large leaf-tailed gecko from Madagascar's northwest; named for herpetologist Albert Günther; declining due to deforestation of its dry forest habitat.

children.name: Uroplatus alluaudi
children.description: A small Uroplatus from northern Madagascar with a short stumpy tail.

children.description: Described from Montagne d'Ambre, Madagascar, with intricate lichen-like patterning.

children.name: Paroedura
children.description: Malagasy ground geckos, terrestrial lizards inhabiting leaf litter and sandy soils of Madagascar.

children.name: Paroedura picta
children.description: Beautifully patterned Madagascar ground gecko with ocelot-like spots; popular in the pet trade; nocturnal hunter of small invertebrates in sandy habitats.

children.name: Paroedura bastardi
children.description: Widespread Madagascar ground gecko in dry and spiny forests; banded pattern provides camouflage in leaf litter; named for French naturalist Bastard.

children.name: Paroedura gracilis
children.description: Slender Madagascar ground gecko from the northwest; more gracile build than P. picta; inhabits dry deciduous forest floor.

children.name: Paroedura masobe
children.description: Striking gecko with large dark eyes surrounded by white scales giving a spectacled appearance; Madagascar endemic restricted to eastern rainforest; 'masobe' means 'big eyes' in Malagasy.

children.name: Paroedura rangei
children.description: Namib Desert specialist with translucent skin and large eyes; strictly nocturnal to avoid extreme daytime heat; shelters in burrows during the day; named for German zoologist Range.

children.name: Paroedura vatha
children.description: Endemic to Andohahela rainforest of southeastern Madagascar; lacks juvenile banding in adults.

children.name: Paroedura tanjaka

children.name: Lepidodactylus
children.description: Scaly-toed geckos of the Pacific region, diverse in Melanesia and Micronesia.

children.name: Lepidodactylus lugubris
children.description: All-female parthenogenetic species spread across the tropics via human transport; small and slender; common on buildings throughout Southeast Asia and Pacific islands.

children.name: Lepidodactylus moestus
children.description: Southeast Asian gecko with distinctive white lateral stripes; found on tree trunks and buildings; widespread from the Philippines through Indonesia.

children.name: Lepidodactylus manni
children.description: Fijian endemic gecko; found in coastal and lowland forests; named for ichthyologist William M. Mann; threatened by habitat loss on Fiji's main islands.

children.name: Lepidodactylus oligoporus
children.description: Bismarck Archipelago endemic in Papua New Guinea; inhabits coastal and lowland tropical forest; poorly studied species known from few collections.

children.name: Lepidodactylus ornatus
children.description: New Caledonian endemic with attractive patterning; found in maquis shrubland and dry forest habitats; threatened by nickel mining and habitat degradation.

children.name: Lepidodactylus pusillus
children.description: One of the smallest Lepidodactylus, found in Solomon Islands coastal coconut palms.

children.name: Lepidodactylus yamii
